speaker: interviewer
text: March the 6th, 1996. Survivor is Fira Silberbach nee Geller. The interviewer is Mary Ziegler. Sydney, Australia, the language is English, and this is tape one.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's Wednesday, the 6th of March, 1996. My name is Mary Ziegler, and I'm interviewing Fira Silberbach in Sydney, Australia. Fira, can we begin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll start with the official part of this interview. Can you tell me your name and how to spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Fira Silberbach, F-I-R-A. The name-- name is S-I-L-B-E-R-B-A-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name of birth is Fira Geller.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell Geller?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-E-- double L-- E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any other names or nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My date of birth is the 20-- 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: Start again. What month?

speaker: interviewee
text: 19th of March, 1921.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So how old are you? How old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I'm 75, or will be. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me the city you were born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was born in Chelm.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: C-H-E-L-E-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Fira, let's talk about Chelm at first. Can you tell me what it was like as a child growing up in Chelm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we-- we were not very rich. We were five children, and we all went to school and finished our schooling. And I had friends and going out and being together. And my sister got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sister? Can you tell me--

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest, Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me the name-- tell me the names of your sisters and your brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister-- the oldest was Sarah, then my brother, Bayer. And I was the middle one. Then was Daniel and Malka.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother and father's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was Moshe. My mother's Hinde. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: When your sister got married, Sarah-- tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: When she got married, my parents were very happy. Later, she had a little boy. It was their first grandchild. And the war broke out. He was seven months old.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Before we get on to the war, just tell me about the Jewish community in Chelm. How big was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I really didn't took notice, but I look up the [INAUDIBLE]. It was about 50-- nearly 50% Jewish population. The rest was Ukrainian, German, and Polacks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We spoke Yiddish and-- a lot. When I grow up, I went-- visit girlfriends. We spoke Polish, and so I-- my Yiddish wasn't so good, so my parents sent me to Bais Yaakov to-- and it was after my proper school-- in the evening courses. And I learned Yiddish. I learned-- and it was to-- from there. I went for two years, and whatever I learned, I still-- it stays with me. And I couldn't [? cope ?] because my parents wasn't so Orthodox to keep up with their children, which came from [? chassidish ?] and the rabbi's daughters. So I couldn't keep up to them when I went out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what job your father had, what his work was?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father has a tailoring business.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother didn't work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your standard of living like in-- in Chelm?

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose middle class.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember your home, what it looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It wasn't very big. We head two rooms and a kitchen. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a-- a flat or a house?

speaker: interviewee
text: A flat, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how big was the building? How--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was-- it was about about 10 tenants. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a beautiful building, [? one of the ?] really because the owner was very wealthy. They were very wealthy. They kept up the-- the place. And it was a beautiful garden, which still stays in my memory.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- Tell me about the garden.

speaker: interviewee
text: The garden-- they kept at that time-- you know, which was very seldom. They had a gardener, a special gardener. And it was kept some trees, which I didn't see-- they used to call it overseas trees, which I discover they have them here in Australia, but I never saw them in Poland, and the roses, and-- and-- and it was really beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it non-Jewish tenants as well as Jewish tenants?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, non-Jewish. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you interact with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was very good. Yeah. It was-- one tenant I remember was a professor from gymnasium, and one was-- he was from taxation. It was-- we-- we lived very, very good with them, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember your secular school, not the Jewish school, but the other one you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that called?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- I-- actually, I went to two. One was [? Konarski, ?] and then I was sent closer-- was [? Yadvika. ?] One-- one was co-ed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Co-ed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one was only for girls. I finished the one which only for girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which did you like better?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was very good. That was the school my sister finished, so that the headmistress, [? you know, ?] we were-- I was well known.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- what was your favorite subject at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I-- I suppose not maths. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: As you were growing up, what--

speaker: interviewee
text: I like history. I liked history.

speaker: interviewer
text: As you were growing up, what-- what were your ambitions? What did you see your future in-- in Chelm in those days?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I can't think of it, what-- what was really my aim. I-- I didn't think about that at that time. You know. Finish school, I had more freedom. And that was just the goal.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what sort of things did you do when you went out after school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- my parents-- I-- because I need a profession, my parents sent me-- it was the last few weeks, I think, to a friend-- to their friend-- to learn some sewing. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, I liked it. Being-- coming from a family, you know, where it was-- there, I couldn't even hold the needles. That's why they sent me to a friend of-- of my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fira, did you have grandparents and uncles and aunts that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had what I remember, one uncle, my mother's brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Adam.

speaker: interviewer
text: His surname?

speaker: interviewee
text: Adam Pomp. And I had my-- from my father-- on my father's side, he still had parents. They lived-- they lived in [? Grabowiec. ?] That's quite-- quite far, and it's a very little place, and I went-- only once I went to visit them. And my-- it was school holiday. My parents send me. We went there by coach, and you travel, I don't remember, from 4 o'clock in the afternoon till 7:00 in the morning, and you have to go through-- through a forest. And one ride, I remember, and the horse knew already because he used to come pick up some goods for-- for shops, and the horse-- the-- the man fell asleep, and I was sitting on there, and in the forest, I was so scared, I used to wake him up. He said, sleep, sleep. And then, that was the first time I went away from my home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was maybe eight, nine. And they sent me there to say for all the vacation, school holidays. But I couldn't-- after three days, I came back, and I-- I-- I was-- so I missed so much, the family, so I never-- I never went without my family. That was the first time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fira, as you were growing up, were you aware of anti-Semitism in-- in Chelm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Not-- not very much because we lived in a-- in a place where mostly it wasn't met too many Jews. You know, Jewish people, they had their quotas, that used to call [POLISH]. And there, only Jews lived. But where I lived, it was mostly Polish people, so I-- we mix-- we mixed well. Only once, I coming home from school, and a little boy poured a bucket of water over me. That was the only incident. Otherwise, I couldn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel-- How I felt? I felt really Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I came home. It was autumn. I came home soaking wet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did your parents react to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- what-- what could they say, you know? It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, Fira. When Hitler came to power in 1933 and anti-Semitism started in-- in Germany, were you aware of that in Chelm, what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we knew because they-- it-- it use-- it came a lot of German people, which they brought to the border, you know, ex-Polish citizen. They broke through the border and let them in, so it was already quite a few in Chelm. And they'd been telling-- but still, they-- they didn't believe is something wrong, and they, you know, they came only dressed whatever they had on, and the Jewish communities start to make collections for them. So that's what all-- all we knew. Otherwise, it-- nobody believed because even my father-- Chelm was under-- in the First World War was under Austria, and in this building where we lived was a lot of Austrians people living. And my father was-- was [INAUDIBLE] at home, I remember he had the big picture with all those people. They used to be together, and he-- he was prizing them, and I remember he used to say, it's impossible. They are good people. It's impossible. He-- they didn't believe it. That's what kept him, because he could-- the family could all got-- got over the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Over the border to where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Russia. And they didn't. He didn't believe. He always showed the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of other people leaving in Chelm? I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: During that time, did people leave? Do you know? Not your family, but other--

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Germans came?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, before--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before?

speaker: interviewer
text: --in-- in those-- in those years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not many. People used to go, you know, to overseas. From my uncle-- actually, I had two-- two-- four cousins-- it was two sons and two daughters-- went to Brazil and to Palestine. So otherwise, people used to go, you know, to America. Though America was very hard to get, so they used to go to other countries.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any youth organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [LAUGHS] Coming from Bais Yaakov, took enough. I couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- do you remember the 1st of September, 1939, when Hitler invaded Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: We remember-- I-- the war broke out. That's when we-- we knew, and-- but it went-- the Germans came-- actually, it was a lot of people running from, you know, from the part-- that part from Poland, and they used to go to-- to-- people were evacuated, running. And we didn't know till the eighth day of-- when the war broke out. That was the first time we experienced a bombing. And what they actually-- what happened-- because the government and all ministers, that-- they-- they passed through Chelm because a lot of Polish ministers ran away to Romania. And that was in the morning. They gathered at the-- how [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: The magistrate?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- where the government is. And-- and most probably, the Germans find out, and they-- but they came in to bomb. And what they bombed-- we lived like [INAUDIBLE], and the next-- next was [INAUDIBLE], which it was a station, so that's what they bombed. It was our street and-- and a-- a station because they thought they-- they will still catch up with them, and they missed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when that bombing was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where I was? I was in the back yard, and over the fence, it was a hospital, and a soldier was lying on the ground, and we were talking to him. It must-- probably he felt the vibration or something, because we didn't know, and he said-- he start to move away, to crawl away. He said, they're coming, they're coming. And when I looked up, I start to count. It was maybe 20 airplanes, and I saw the bombs falling. But for me, I-- I couldn't understand what it is-- what happened just across the road. A bomb fell into a building and was killed-- four people were killed. It was in the afternoon. And-- and then we start to run to the cellar. In the cellar, we were about 30 people in the cellar. And when it was already over, we came-- I came-- it was already evening, and I came out to-- to-- to the front of the street, and I saw this house burning, and people was start-- with water-- buckets of water to hand over. I got-- it hit me then, and I got so hysteric, and I started to run to-- my sister who lived on the other part of the city. When I came there, I don't know how this happened. My parents were already there. I don't-- I don't even know how it happened. And I came in there, and I found it. And that-- that was the first-- the eighth day of-- of-- that we start to feel. Later on, when we-- my parents collect all the children, and we left town. We walked-- we went where I was on-- on this holiday about-- I-- I don't know how many miles, about 10 miles. A whole night, we walked, and we came there because we didn't want to see-- to be in the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Only food. It was before Yontif. My mother prepared all of the foods. Only food, we took it. We left everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you going? Tell me the name of the town.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And why-- why did you go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it-- it was-- you know, it wasn't the military's object. And there, every morning, we used to go out in the forest, and the airplanes used to fly over. And I was-- and we made up that in the forest that my father was sitting with the two children, my mother separate so in case-- we-- we were under this impression that the airplane could spot us. You know, that's how I-- I-- I was terrified. And we stayed there. That was about 10 days. And then it was quiet, and they-- the war-- we came back-- was ahead of Yom Kippur. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your-- what did you find when you came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came back, my-- it-- my mother had beautiful plants, a parapet in-- in the-- it's a-- on the windows, and all of the plants were dead. But we found out that just a bomb, which didn't explode, was over the fence. That's what-- otherwise, the house would have been-- and all of the windows were-- have all of the ceilings have been. So we came back [? ahead of ?] Yom Kippur. My mother went out in the market, and that was-- and she prepared-- [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember what she made potatoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] And then, they went off to-- to shul. I-- I was sitting home. That was the last holiday.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what-- what sort of things did you talk about when you got back, about what was going to happen, Fira? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember they-- they went to shul. And we went to our friend, you know, myself and the two younger, to be close to the shul, and that in the afternoon. A big shooting came that the Germans were already not far, and that was the last of them. And-- and nobody came, no Germans. The Polish army was dissolved, and nothing. And it was a lot of, you know, Polish people shot, whomever they had a grudge or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we used-- my father used to put us on the floor near the window because there was a lot of shooting in between so that it wouldn't hit us. And then, four days later, it was-- it was Sukkot. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I was in center in the city, and suddenly, people start to scream and run away and hide in the passages in the backyard. And then, when I-- we came out-- and Chelm is rather hilly. All of the streets go up on a-- And then came-- from up the hill came tanks, four tanks, and people start to run [INAUDIBLE] them. What's happened? It was the first four Russian tanks. The Russians came in. And, you know, it was young people, Communists, they must probably knew. Not that they knew-- they'd been waiting from the outskirts of town, and they've been sitting on the tanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so it was a very happy occasion. They went through the whole [INAUDIBLE] straight. Also, it was collect the Polish army, people which were already, you know, coming from the front going home-- they caught them and made a army-- I think that was [INAUDIBLE] army, because in Warsaw was still fighting. And so when the Red-- when the Red Army came in on the tanks, this-- they had them already-- the Polish army was already on the train to move, and they stopped to shoot to stop them, and they shoot back. It was-- two Russian soldiers were killed. And so the whole evening, people were happy. The whole night, people-- you know, they preferred the Russians than the Germans, and so that's-- you know, especially in Chelm, it-- the First World War was also Russian people. And that was-- and they went on. It was-- took two-- two weeks. They were two weeks in Chelm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- did you feel any threat as a Jew in those-- in that time? You know, when the war just started, did you feel any need to-- to leave Chelm at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not that time, not at all. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you weren't feeling any threat as being a Jewess?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. When-- you think, when Poland was in Poland-- look, I knew I was Jewish, so I didn't have pretenses.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when-- when the Germans invaded and everything--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They--

speaker: interviewer
text: The local population didn't threaten you in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no. So the-- the Russians were two weeks, and then they start to move out when they made the-- the border. So the border was behind Chelm. The River Bug was the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, well, I think we'll stop here.
